ground_truth.title: Would you ever consider a career in Early Years Childcare? Why or why not?
ground_truth.author: Mod Sharon
ground_truth.main_content:
Mod Sharon
28/11/2025 at 7:07 pm

We'd love to hear your thoughts.

If you've ever thought about working in Early Years (nurseries, preschool, childcare roles) — what would make you consider it?

And what would put you off?

Things like flexibility, hours, training, pay, stress levels, confidence, or anything else — all views welcome.

Thank you for sharing.

The Netmums Team

Judith C(157)
28/11/2025 at 7:16 pm

Hi

So the answer for me would be no.

I did consider a role in childcare and did work for a short time as a teaching assistant in a rural primary school.

I have no teaching qualifications but I was often left looking after a 30+ kids and teaching them too - I was expected to complete all necessary training in my own time - unpaid.

I left and got a job in Tesco's - much less stress, much less responsibility and much more money each week!

No brainer!

Shame though as such could be a lovely career but not valued

Otterlie B(197)
28/11/2025 at 7:22 pm

Hi

I did work in childcare early years once - I loved it as its such an important time for a childs development. However I left after a while as the volume of work / prep etc needed spilled out into my home life and private time.

I was loosing out on spending time with my own family so I made the choice to leave and get a job where my working hours were fixed and I knew when I walked out the door I was done for the day.

I think my kids were happier too once I was more available to them in the evenings too

Indi I(164)
28/11/2025 at 7:28 pm

Hi

I'd love a career working with the little one's, such a privilege but the stress of it would be too much.

I'd worry about all the rules and regulations and the safeguarding risks of working with small people and how vulnerable you are these days working with kids - not for the money you get paid in return.

I think its more of a vocation that a job with the wages!

Georgina B(130)
28/11/2025 at 7:43 pm

Hi

I work in childcare and I have done for years.

I only work part time 10-2 and it has fitted in around my own children saving on child care costs. I have a husband who has a very well paid job so my job was just something I could do around the kids and bring in some pocket money.

Without my husbands wage this would not have been possible. I love my job but I don't do it for the money or the career ladder.

Chris W(183)
28/11/2025 at 7:55 pm

It sounds ideal in theory, all those holidays and great work hours but I'm sure it is very stressful and tiring. The thought of day with 30 children in my care and then coming home to my own sounds like a nightmare.

I think if it was better paid and you had help in the classroom, plus time to do prep and marking so you aren't working at home, then maybe it would be for me.

Elle D(111)
28/11/2025 at 8:00 pm

It would be my ideal job but I am not qualified to do it and it was a very long time ago that I sat any exams. I am not confident that my maths and english skills would be up to date-they seem to teach things very differently now and I'd be worried I would teach something wrong.

I would also be frightened of dealing with angry parents, I don't deal well with conflict.

Sarah D(112)
28/11/2025 at 8:42 pm

I would love to work with little children. I love their innocence humour and watching them grow.

What puts me off though are the parents. I have seen some really angry ones demanding their children are cared for 'their' way even though they remain happy and cared for in another way when I have dropped mine off.

The poor staff are only doing their best, I feel sorry for them sometimes.

I'm not saying all parents are monsters but a lot of them can really be cruel and frightening.

So a reluctant no from me ;)

nicola p(273)
29/11/2025 at 8:37 pm

Honestly no. I used to volunteer at my sons primary school and I realised fairly quickly that I do not have the patience. I also don't think I have the skills. There's been a few times I've tried to teach a fairly simple task and it's just not something I seem to be able to manage. Massive respect to anyone that does do it for a career. They are hugely underpaid for what is a highly skilled job.

Tracey S(122)
30/11/2025 at 2:55 pm

Its makes me a bit sad reading this.

I used to Childmind, I really enjoyed it. It was the job you could do while looking after your own kids too if you had the right child ratio.

Once the role changed where you had to jump through so many hoops from the ofsted inspectors and the early years curriculum i just stopped. It was just TOO much paper work and out of hours work.

It ceased to become enjoyable and the pay just not worth it. I do agree childminders have to be monitored for the safety of the children in their care but childminders were never meant to be mini nurseries

Suzy W(178)
30/11/2025 at 3:01 pm

I'd be up for it I think. Be such a privilege to work with your children and help them grow. I think it would also be a lot of fun too.

What puts me off is the demands of the job though and I'm not sure it would pay enough as I am a single mum. Not sure if there is any progression for this either, so you'd be stuck on the same wage ? I maybe wrong though but looking at what other people has said here I'm probably not far off the point!!

Jack J(128)
30/11/2025 at 3:09 pm

Hi

My wife works in early years but she is on mat leave right now. We've had a bit of a tough time with PND etc so its not been easy so far.

She is not back to work until after the summer next year and we have already had the conversation of whether or not she will go back. She was under work stress before she started her mat leave, and the extra work she had to do out of hours may be just too much for her now we have a baby and she would never want the children under her care to be compromised as a result.

She may go back as she loves the profession but the fact we have had the conversation does suggest its already on her mind.

Charlie R(153)
01/12/2025 at 6:49 pm

I think I would like to work with children.

I think if you have had children (I have 3) it puts you in a really good position to look after others. You just 'get it' and understand life from a parents perspective too.

So much has changed with 'gentle parenting' and lots of new ideas etc you'd have to make sure you were continually updating yourself though so not sure if would have to wait for mine to be a bit older to be qualified enough.

Not sure how you go about getting the training to even apply these days - but an idea for the future maybe for me :)
